title: The Prince
description: A hard-edged manual of political rule, calculation, and statecraft under unstable conditions.
descriptionParagraphs: The Prince is a compact treatise on how rulers gain, keep, and lose power in unstable political conditions. Machiavelli is less interested in moral perfection than in contingency, necessity, reputation, and the difficult choices made by leaders who operate in a world of enemies, shifting alliances, and unreliable fortune.

Its lasting fascination comes from the way it strips politics of comforting illusions without reducing it to simple cynicism. Machiavelli asks what effective rule requires when virtue in the classical or Christian sense does not reliably preserve the state. The result is a book that still frames debates about realism, reason of state, and the relation between ethics and power.

cover.alt: The Prince cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A hard-edged manual of political rule, calculation, and statecraft under unstable conditions.
banningParagraphs: The Prince entered censorship debates as a political treatise associated with statecraft, power, and anti clericalism. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around anti clericalism and political danger.

The earliest event currently captured here is early modern period in Papal States, where Catholic authorities in the Papal States forbade circulation. The treatise's reputation for amoral politics and anticlericalism made it a target. This is a useful reminder that governments and churches often censored political realism itself.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
